text: Hello, my name is Dieter Pohl. I'm one of the surgeons together with Dr. Blumenthal and Dr. Christian here at Roger Williams Medical Center in the weight loss surgery program. And today I will talk to you about the laparoscopic pancreatic diversion with duodenal switch surgery, also called in the short version duodenal switch or even shorter DS.

If you want to hear more about the surgery you can make an appointment with one of us for zero one five two one six three one zero or go to our website loseweightri.com. The patient in this video is a forty five year old man with a body mass index of fifty.

The surgeon displays the abdomen with six marked incisions, four quarter-inch and two half-inch. Cannulas are introduced through these incisions for instrument access. Inside the body, yellow fat and red muscle are visible. The surgeon navigates the right upper part of the abdomen, identifying the grayish colon structure on the right side. The right colon is visible in the left upper corner, and the surgeon identifies the small intestines, measuring back from the colon.

The surgeon uses graspers marked every four inches to measure ten feet of the small intestine. At the midpoint, five feet from the starting point, a clip is placed to mark the location for future intestinal connections. This marked section will later facilitate digestion and absorption processes. The surgeon then marks this spot with a stitch and sutures it to the abdominal wall for easy identification during subsequent reattachment procedures.

The surgeon uses a liver retractor to lift the brownish liver, revealing the pinkish stomach on the right. The laparoscopic view shows limited space between the stomach and liver, approximately less than an inch wide. The surgeon identifies and measures back from the end of the stomach, marking a spot about four to five centimeters (approximately two inches) for later use in freeing up the stomach.

The surgeon detaches the stomach from surrounding structures, including the diaphragm, revealing the freed stomach. An endoscope is introduced into the stomach to guide the placement of a stapler, which positions three rows of titanium staples and simultaneously cuts the tissue, creating a sleeve gastrectomy. The separated stomach is visible, with the sleeve stomach on the left and the excised portion on the right.

The surgeon continues stapling and cutting along the endoscope in the stomach, creating a sleeve gastrectomy. A small bleeder is visible at the bottom, which is managed with stitches or clips. The duodenum is detached from surrounding fat, creating a tunnel behind it, with the pancreas visible below. The duodenum is pulled away from the bile duct and gallbladder, and a device is used to finish the tunnel. Bleeding is controlled with stitches, staples, or clips, and the duodenum is stapled about an inch and a half below the stomach, with bleeding stopped.

The surgeon creates a hole in the duodenum using electrocautery to connect it to the marked section of the intestines. Yellow bile fluid is visible as it emerges from the duodenum. Due to excessive fat, the surgeon removes some fat from the colon to facilitate the connection. The marked intestine is detached from the belly wall and positioned ten feet before the colon. The surgeon uses a stapler to create a wide opening for the connection, ensuring no narrowing or stricturing occurs.

The surgeon closes the stapler on the intestinal connection, placing staples on both sides. The gallbladder is visible in the background. As the stapler opens, the inside of the intestines is exposed, showing bile. The surgeon reinforces the corners with stitches to prevent leakage from the large opening, ensuring a secure seal.

The surgeon is performing a duodenal switch, cutting the section where pancreatic and bile juices enter the duodenum. The duodenum is connected to the intestines, with stitches placed in the top and bottom corners to ensure proper healing and tension relief. On the right side, light shines through the sleeve stomach, while the intestines on the left are occluded with a grasper. Water is applied for a leak test.

The surgeon performs a leak test by blowing air into the sleeve stomach, checking for air bubbles underwater to ensure no leaks. Once confirmed, the surgeon proceeds to connect the intestines that transport food from the sleeve to the colon. Using a stapler, holes are made on each side, and the two intestinal segments are connected, ensuring a wide opening to prevent narrowing or kinking.

The surgeon closes a small opening in the intestine using a stapler and reinforces the corners with stitches to prevent leakage and tension. The connection allows food to flow from the right side bottom intestine to the left side bottom intestine, then to the colon, where absorption occurs over the last five feet.

The surgeon places stitches to prevent bowel blockages, addressing potential internal hernias caused by intestine movement through gaps between layers of fat. The detached stomach is placed into a plastic bag to prevent contamination during removal through a half-inch incision. The surgeon uses a finger to ensure no air leaks before switching out the incisions with stitches to prevent hernias. The abdomen is washed, and cannulas are removed, revealing six incisions.
So again, this is our team. If you're interested in learning about the surgery, go to our website, loseweightri.com or call the office and make an appointment. Thank you.
